blurb_en:
Midjourney, the company that taught the world to generate images with words, announced Wednesday it is also now building a device for imaging your body — specifically a full-body ultrasound scanner you will use while standing in a spa.
- The device uses 8,960 ultrasound transducers to produce a 3D body map in roughly 60 seconds, with no radiation and no MRI-style magnets
- The company plans to open a Midjourney Spa in San Francisco's Union Square in late 2027, with hot tubs, saunas, and cold plunges alongside 10 scanners
- The prototype currently takes about 20 minutes — the 60-second figure is the target, not the current reality
- No peer-reviewed clinical data published yet; resolution claims versus MRI are tested on synthetic phantoms, not real patients
- Long-term goal: 50,000 scanners deployed, one billion scans per month by 2031
- For imaging professionals, the reconstruction pipeline is volumetric AI inference from acoustic data — the core computer-vision problem is the same as photogrammetry, the medium is not
blurb_zh:
Midjourney——那家让你用文字生成图像的公司——周三宣布正在建造全身超声扫描仪，并计划将其置于一家水疗中心供人使用。
- 设备配备8960个换能器，无辐射无磁场，目标60秒生成3D全身图像
- 计划2027年底在旧金山联合广场开设水疗中心，设有热水浴缸、桑拿和冷水浴池，旁边摆10台扫描仪
- 当前原型机实际运行需20分钟，60秒只是目标
- 目标：到2031年部署5万台，每月完成10亿次扫描
- 对影像行业人士而言：这本质上是声学数据的体积AI推理——核心计算机视觉问题与摄影测量学相同，介质不同
verification_notes: Confirmed via The Register, Gizmodo, TechTimes (June 18), AuntMinnie, Latent.space, and midjourney.com/medical/blogpost (official announcement). TechTimes notes prototype runs 20 minutes, not 60 seconds. Butterfly Networks cited as hardware partner in AuntMinnie.

blurb_en:
Cornell Tech researchers published a preprint this spring showing that 13 words inserted into a Reddit-style public comment can reliably redirect AI deep-research agents toward scams or sponsored recommendations.
- The attack is called WARP — Web Agent Retrieval Poisoning — and it exploits the fact that AI agents trust whatever they retrieve from the open web
- No hacking required: any user who can post a comment on a public platform can place the poison
- Researchers ran everything in sandboxes; they did not poison the live internet, but the vulnerability is architectural — the model cannot distinguish a poisoned source from a legitimate one
- AI search was supposed to be harder to game than keyword SEO; the paper suggests it is at least as easy and in some cases easier
- Implication for creative professionals: any AI research tool recommending software, hardware, or workflows is trusting the same content layer that can be poisoned
blurb_zh:
康奈尔大学研究人员今春发表预印论文：在Reddit风格的公开评论中植入13个单词，就能让AI深度研究代理系统性地转向推荐诈骗或赞助内容。
- 攻击方式命名为WARP（网络代理检索投毒），利用AI代理默认信任开放网络内容的特性
- 无需任何黑客技术——任何可在公开平台发帖的用户均可实施
- 研究者仅在沙盒环境中测试，未在真实网络投毒，但漏洞属于架构层面
- AI搜索原本被认为比传统SEO更难操控——该论文表明并非如此
verification_notes: Confirmed via 404 Media and Tom's Guide (June 15 publication per search result metadata). Cornell Tech preprint by Tingwei Zhang, Harold Triedman, Vitaly Shmatikov titled 'Deep-Research Agents Can Be Poisoned via User-Generated Content'. Attack named WARP (Web Agent Retrieval Poisoning). Cross-referenced via Windows Forum thread and Yahoo Tech.

blurb_en:
SpaceX revealed the AI1 on June 9 — a 70-meter orbital data center satellite designed to process AI workloads from orbit — four days before the company's Nasdaq IPO under ticker SPCX.
- 150 kW peak compute, roughly 70 kW per ton; the compute payload is modular and chipmaker-agnostic to avoid Nvidia lock-in
- Spans wider than a Boeing 747 wing-to-wing; a deployable liquid-radiator array handles thermal management in the vacuum of space
- Prototype launches in early 2027; SpaceX's roadmap targets 1 GW of orbital AI compute capacity by late 2027
- Elon Musk called it 'much simpler than a Starlink satellite,' which is either reassuring or an indication of what Starlink's complexity actually looks like
- No independent economic analysis yet supports the claim that orbital compute is cost-competitive with ground data centers at scale
- For imaging professionals: at 1 GW of orbital compute, this is potential infrastructure for real-time AI processing of satellite and aerial imagery without the round-trip latency of ground relay
blurb_zh:
SpaceX于6月9日公布AI1——一颗长70米的轨道数据中心卫星，将AI计算推向轨道，时机精准卡在公司纳斯达克上市前四天。
- 峰值计算功率150千瓦，约70千瓦/吨，计算载荷模块化、不绑定特定芯片供应商
- 翼展超过波音747；液体散热阵列解决太空真空中的散热难题
- 原型机计划2027年初发射，路线图目标是2027年底达到1吉瓦轨道算力
- 马斯克称其'比星链卫星简单得多'——对于一颗需要防微陨石护甲的航天器，这话颇耐人寻味
verification_notes: Confirmed via Tom's Hardware, TechTimes (June 10), Quartz (June 10 article qz.com/spacex-ai1-satellite-orbital-data-center-ipo-061026), Data Center Dynamics, ScienceDaily. Original announcement: Elon Musk video on X.com June 9 2026. No TechCrunch/Ars/Wired tier-1 article found, but consistent technical specs across 5+ secondaries. SpaceX IPO (SPCX) confirmed via separate existing story in DB (Burn Rate, June 12).

blurb_en:
Qualcomm is in early talks to acquire Tenstorrent, Jim Keller's AI chip startup, for between eight and ten billion dollars, according to The Information.
- Tenstorrent was last valued at $2.6 billion in its December 2024 Series D — this deal would represent a 3-4x premium
- The strategic rationale is RISC-V: Tenstorrent's chips use the open-source instruction set as an alternative to Arm and x86, reducing licensing costs across the stack
- Jim Keller previously designed chips at Intel, AMD, Apple, and Tesla — his track record is the primary asset being acquired
- Neither company will comment; the talks could still fall through
- If it closes, Qualcomm enters the datacenter AI chip market with a credible architecture; if it doesn't, Tenstorrent remains one of the few independent challengers to Nvidia
blurb_zh:
据The Information报道，高通正就收购吉姆·凯勒创办的AI芯片初创公司Tenstorrent展开初步谈判，估值介于80至100亿美元。
- Tenstorrent在2024年12月D轮融资时估值为26亿美元，此次溢价3-4倍
- 战略赌注在于RISC-V：Tenstorrent芯片采用开源指令集，可替代Arm和x86，降低授权成本
- 凯勒曾主导设计英特尔、AMD、苹果和特斯拉的芯片——其架构经验是核心标的
- 双方均未置评，谈判仍可能告吹
verification_notes: Broken by The Information; confirmed by The Register (June 16), Yahoo Finance, Data Center Dynamics, GuruFocus (multiple articles). Qualcomm and Tenstorrent both declined to comment per The Register and Yahoo Finance. Tenstorrent $2.6B Series D valuation confirmed via multiple sources. Jim Keller as founder confirmed.

blurb_en:
The Third Circuit Court of Appeals heard oral arguments on June 11 in Thomson Reuters v. ROSS Intelligence — the first U.S. appeals court review of whether using copyrighted material to train AI constitutes fair use.
- The district court ruled in 2025 that ROSS infringed more than 2,000 Thomson Reuters Westlaw headnotes; ROSS argues its internal training use was transformative
- The three-judge panel (Restrepo, Montgomery-Reeves, Bove) heard 30 minutes of argument; no ruling has been issued
- The specific dispute is narrow — short editorial summaries of judicial opinions — but the principle if affirmed would ripple across every AI training dataset built from copyrighted databases
- A separate fair-use precedent from the same circuit (ASTM v. UpCodes) hangs over the case; both parties cited it differently in supplemental briefs the panel requested
- No timeline on a decision; Third Circuit rulings typically come within months of argument
blurb_zh:
第三巡回上诉法院于6月11日就汤森路透诉ROSS Intelligence案进行口头辩论——这是美国首个就AI训练是否构成合理使用展开的联邦上诉级审查。
- 下级法院2025年裁定ROSS侵权了2000余条Westlaw标题注释；ROSS主张内部训练使用具有转换性
- 三名法官合议庭听取30分钟辩论，尚未作出裁决
- 该案争议点较窄——仅涉及简短的司法意见编辑摘要——但判决一旦确立将波及所有基于版权数据库构建的AI训练集
verification_notes: Oral argument confirmed via CourtListener public docket (case 25-2153), chatgptiseatingtheworld.com (June 10 livestream announcement), aicounsel.substack.com (argument analysis), TechJacksSolutions (June 11 coverage). No Tier-1 outlet coverage found. Panel composition (Restrepo, Montgomery-Reeves, Bove) confirmed via docket. Prior DB entry 'Case Study' covers April 14 scheduling article — this is distinct (actual argument date). No ruling issued yet.

blurb_en:
Match Group — the company behind Tinder, Hinge, OkCupid, and Match.com — released a survey this week showing 47 percent of U.S. singles view AI in romantic relationships negatively.
- The same survey found 64 percent use AI to write better dating profiles and keep conversations going
- Two in five singles say they would refuse to date someone who uses an AI companion app; among women aged 18-24, the figure rises to 51 percent
- The finding is awkward for Match Group, which has invested heavily in AI across all its products
- Users draw a consistent line: AI as a tool to improve human romance is fine; AI as a substitute for human romance is not
- For imaging professionals: the same dynamic applies to AI retouching — clients want the result, not a detailed account of the pipeline
blurb_zh:
Match Group——旗下拥有Tinder、Hinge、OkCupid等应用——本周发布调查：47%的美国单身人士对恋爱中的AI持负面态度。
- 同一调查显示64%的人用AI撰写更好的约会档案和维持对话
- 五分之二的受访者不愿与使用AI伴侣应用的人约会；18-24岁女性中这一比例升至51%
- 对大量押注AI功能的Match Group而言，这份数据颇为尴尬
- 用户划定的界限清晰：AI辅助人类恋爱可以，AI取代人类恋爱不行
verification_notes: Confirmed via TechCrunch (June 18) and Axios (June 18). Match Group partnered with Ipsos on survey of 1,000 U.S. singles aged 18-39. 47% negative view confirmed across both outlets. 64% AI profile use stat confirmed via TechCrunch. 51% women 18-24 refusing AI companion app partners confirmed via Global Dating Insights and Mezha.net.
